questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: automation / loss / leads / to / jobs / of
options: Leads to loss of jobs automation
Loss of jobs automation leads to
Automation leads to loss of jobs
Jobs loss of to leads automation
questions.solution: Automation leads to loss of jobs

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: Underground mining is one of the most hazardous occupations in the world. The primary risks include roof falls, gas explosions, flooding, and ventilation failures. In India, the Mines Act of 1952 and the Coal Mines Regulations of 2017 govern safety standards. Modern technology has significantly improved mine safety through real-time monitoring systems, gas detectors, and automated equipment. Despite these advances, safety remains a critical concern, and regular training programs are mandatory for all mine workers. The introduction of continuous miners and longwall mining methods has increased both productivity and safety.

What has improved mine safety according to the passage?
options: Real-time monitoring systems and gas detectors
questions.solution: Real-time monitoring systems and gas detectors

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: quality / compromising / without / costs / reduce / should
options: Costs should reduce without compromising quality
Quality compromising without reduce costs should
Compromising quality without costs should reduce
Should reduce costs without compromising quality
questions.solution: Costs should reduce without compromising quality

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: Coal is the most abundant fossil fuel in India and plays a crucial role in the country's energy mix. India has the fourth-largest coal reserves in the world, with an estimated 344 billion tonnes. Coal India Limited (CIL) is the largest coal-producing company globally, accounting for about 82% of India's coal output. However, concerns about environmental degradation and climate change have prompted a shift towards cleaner energy sources like solar and wind power. The government has set a target of achieving 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030.

Which of the following is mentioned as a concern related to coal usage?

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: solve / team / the / to / problem / together / worked
options: The team worked together to solve the problem
Worked together the team to solve the problem
Together the team worked to solve the problem
To solve the problem the team together worked
questions.solution: The team worked together to solve the problem

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: India's energy demand is projected to grow at 3-4% annually over the next decade. While coal remains the dominant source, accounting for about 55% of installed capacity, renewable energy is expanding rapidly. Solar power capacity has grown from just 2.6 GW in 2014 to over 70 GW in 2024. Wind power contributes another 45 GW. The government's Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for solar manufacturing aims to reduce dependence on imports. Nuclear power, with 7.5 GW capacity, and hydropower, with 47 GW, are other important components of India's energy portfolio.

India's installed nuclear power capacity is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: workers / the / demanded / conditions / working / better
options: Demanded the workers better working conditions
Working conditions demanded the workers better
The workers demanded better working conditions
Better working conditions the workers demanded
questions.solution: The workers demanded better working conditions

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Gondwana coal fields of eastern India contain the country's best quality coal. These fields stretch across Jharkhand, West Bengal, Odisha, and Chhattisgarh. The coal found here is primarily bituminous, which has high calorific value and low ash content. In contrast, the Tertiary coal fields of northeastern India contain lignite and sub-bituminous coal. Coal grading is based on ash content, with higher grades having lower ash percentages. Washeries are used to reduce ash content and improve coal quality before it is supplied to power plants and industries.

Coal grading is primarily based on:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: country / infrastructure / of / development / backbone / the / is
options: The backbone of country development is infrastructure
Development of country backbone is infrastructure
Country development backbone is the infrastructure
Infrastructure is the backbone of country development
questions.solution: Infrastructure is the backbone of country development

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: India's energy demand is projected to grow at 3-4% annually over the next decade. While coal remains the dominant source, accounting for about 55% of installed capacity, renewable energy is expanding rapidly. Solar power capacity has grown from just 2.6 GW in 2014 to over 70 GW in 2024. Wind power contributes another 45 GW. The government's Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for solar manufacturing aims to reduce dependence on imports. Nuclear power, with 7.5 GW capacity, and hydropower, with 47 GW, are other important components of India's energy portfolio.

Coal accounts for about what percentage of India's installed capacity?

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: government / measures / safety / implemented / has / stringent
options: Stringent safety measures government has implemented
Government has implemented stringent safety measures
Safety measures stringent implemented has government
Implemented has government stringent safety measures
questions.solution: Government has implemented stringent safety measures

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: Coal is the most abundant fossil fuel in India and plays a crucial role in the country's energy mix. India has the fourth-largest coal reserves in the world, with an estimated 344 billion tonnes. Coal India Limited (CIL) is the largest coal-producing company globally, accounting for about 82% of India's coal output. However, concerns about environmental degradation and climate change have prompted a shift towards cleaner energy sources like solar and wind power. The government has set a target of achieving 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030.

The government's renewable energy target by 2030 is ____ GW.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: modern / has / technology / efficiency / improved / significantly
options: Modern technology has improved efficiency significantly
Efficiency has modern technology improved significantly
Improved efficiency has significantly modern technology
Technology modern has efficiency improved significantly
questions.solution: Modern technology has improved efficiency significantly

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Gondwana coal fields of eastern India contain the country's best quality coal. These fields stretch across Jharkhand, West Bengal, Odisha, and Chhattisgarh. The coal found here is primarily bituminous, which has high calorific value and low ash content. In contrast, the Tertiary coal fields of northeastern India contain lignite and sub-bituminous coal. Coal grading is based on ash content, with higher grades having lower ash percentages. Washeries are used to reduce ash content and improve coal quality before it is supplied to power plants and industries.

The coal found in Gondwana fields is primarily:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: globalisation / economies / has / world / around / the / transformed
options: Around the world globalisation has transformed economies
Globalisation has transformed economies around the world
Transformed economies globalisation has around the world
Economies around the world has globalisation transformed
questions.solution: Globalisation has transformed economies around the world

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late 18th century, was powered largely by coal. Steam engines, fuelled by coal, drove factories, railways, and ships, transforming economies and societies. The demand for coal led to the expansion of mining operations across Europe and America. However, the social cost was high, with poor working conditions, child labour, and environmental pollution. The legacy of the Industrial Revolution continues to influence modern industrial practices and environmental policies. Today, many former mining regions are being revitalised through diversification and clean energy initiatives.

The word 'legacy' in the passage most nearly means:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: digital / has / way / the / changed / communication / we
options: Communication digital has changed the way we
We communicate way the has changed digital technology
Digital technology has changed the way we communicate
The way we communicate has changed digital technology
questions.solution: Digital technology has changed the way we communicate

questions.stem: The teacher was satisfied ____ the results.

questions.stem: Antonym of JEOPARDIZE:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: FIFO (First-In First-Out):
options: Synchronous or asynchronous. Read/write pointers. Full/empty flags. Gray address coding
questions.solution: FIFO: async FIFO for CDC. Binary pointer → Gray code (1-bit change). Depth calculation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Routh-Hurwitz: s⁴+1s³+3s²+2s+4=0. System is:
questions.solution: All coefficients positive and Routh array checks needed.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 14 kHz tone is sampled at 12 kHz. The alias frequency that appears in the baseband is:
questions.solution: Alias = |f − fs| = |14 − 12| = 2 kHz (since f exceeds fs).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Stub matching:
options: Uses shorted/open stub of adjustable length to cancel reactance
questions.solution: Stub: single (series or shunt), double, triple. Smith chart used for design. Shunt stub is common.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A BJT with β = 200 has a base current of 40 µA. Its collector current is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A photodiode is normally operated in:
questions.solution: Reverse-biased photodiodes produce a current proportional to incident light.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Interrupt — priority and nesting:
options: Higher priority interrupts lower priority ones. Daisy chain or interrupt controller (NVIC)
questions.solution: Interrupt nesting: save/restore context. GIC (generic interrupt controller) in ARM. Interrupt latency.

questions.subject: Electronics and Telecommunication Engineering
questions.stem: DTC (direct torque control): controls:
options: Torque and flux directly without current loops
questions.solution: DTC: estimates torque and flux from measured V and I. Hysteresis controllers select voltage vectors directly.

questions.subject: Electronics and Telecommunication Engineering
questions.stem: Prony's method: used for:
options: Modeling signal as sum of exponentials
questions.solution: Prony: fits signal to sum of complex exponentials. Used in system identification, modal analysis. Requires solving linear equations.

questions.subject: Electronics and Telecommunication Engineering
questions.stem: Quarter-wave transformer matching: characteristic impedance Z₁ =
questions.solution: Quarter-wave: Z₁ = √(Z₀·ZL). Transforms load ZL to source Z₀. Only exact at design frequency.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal equivalent of the binary number 1110₂ is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A PN-junction diode conducts appreciably only when it is:
questions.solution: Forward bias lowers the barrier and allows current; reverse bias blocks it (except small leakage).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An 10-bit ADC provides how many quantization levels?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Hall effect measures:
options: Carrier concentration and type (N or P)
questions.solution: Hall coefficient R_H = 1/(qn). Sign → carrier type. Combined with σ → mobility.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Gray code advantage:
options: Only one bit changes between adjacent codes
questions.solution: Gray code: adjacent values differ by 1 bit. Reduces errors in position encoding.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about diodes is INCORRECT?
options: A Zener operates in reverse breakdown
Reverse leakage current is very small
A diode conducts in forward bias
An ideal diode conducts fully in reverse bias
questions.solution: An ideal diode blocks in reverse bias; it conducts in forward bias.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A half-adder produces which two outputs?
questions.solution: A half-adder gives Sum = A⊕B and Carry = A·B.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Microprocessor vs microcontroller:
options: uP: CPU only (memory, I/O external). uC: integrated CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O + timers
questions.solution: uC: 8051, PIC, AVR, ARM Cortex-M. Single chip solution. Lower power, lower cost.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Maxwell's equation ∇×H = J + ∂D/∂t is:
questions.solution: Ampere-Maxwell: curl of H = J + displacement current (∂D/∂t). Modified Ampere's law.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Mod-n counter:
options: Counts 0 to n-1 then resets. Decade counter (0-9) = mod-10
questions.solution: Mod: truncate natural count. Clear or preset. 74LS90 (decade), 74LS93 (4-bit).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Watchdog timer:
options: Prevents system hang. Must be periodically reset. If timeout → system reset
questions.solution: Watchdog: independent timer. Windowed watchdog: must reset within time window. Hardware/software.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: VSWR (Voltage Standing Wave Ratio):
questions.solution: VSWR = 1 (perfect match). VSWR = ∞ (open/short). Γ = (Z_L - Z₀)/(Z_L + Z₀).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 6-bit binary counter counts from:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Return loss = -20log₁₀(|Γ|). For Γ=0.1:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the logic circuit shown, Y = (A AND B) OR C with A = 0, B = 1, C = 0. The output Y is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The stack in 8085:
options: LIFO memory (PUSH stores, POP retrieves). SP points to top of stack
questions.solution: Stack: PUSH rp (decrement SP, store). POP rp (load, increment SP). Used for CALL/RET, interrupts.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Analog multiplier — Gilbert cell:
options: V_out = K·V_x·V_y. Translinear principle. Four-quadrant multiplication
questions.solution: Gilbert: differential pairs. Balanced modulator. Used in mixers, amplitude modulation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The AM waveform's envelope (shown) has Amax = 10 V and Amin = 2 V. The modulation index is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Gibbs phenomenon:
options: Overshoot near discontinuities when reconstructing from Fourier series (doesn't decrease with more terms)
questions.solution: Gibbs: ~9% overshoot at jump discontinuity. Finite terms → ringing. Does not go to zero.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: SSB modulation:
options: One sideband only. Half bandwidth. Power efficient. Requires carrier reinsertion
questions.solution: SSB: USB or LSB. Bandwidth = message bandwidth. Used in HF communications.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Nyquist sampling theorem: minimum sampling rate for signal bandwidth B Hz:
questions.solution: Nyquist rate = 2×bandwidth. Aliasing occurs if f_s < 2B.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: State machine design steps:
options: State diagram → state table → state assignment → FF type → excitation → output logic
questions.solution: FSM: sequence detector, controller. State encoding: binary, Gray, one-hot.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Mealy state machine output depends on:
questions.solution: Mealy: output = f(state, input). Moore: output = f(state) only.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many bits are needed to encode 32 distinct quantization levels?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inverting amp: Rf=470.0kΩ, Rin=22.0kΩ, Vin=0.5V. Vout:

questions.stem: Phase margin of a system at gain crossover frequency ω_gc=5 rad/s with phase=-140°:

questions.stem: A 4-bit binary counter counts from:

questions.stem: RC low-pass filter: R=1kΩ, C=1000nF. Cutoff frequency f_c≈

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inverting amp: Rf=220.0kΩ, Rin=22.0kΩ. Gain:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An active filter uses:
questions.solution: Active filter: op-amp + resistors + capacitors. Advantages: no inductors, gain, high Z_in, low Z_out.

questions.subject: Electronics and Telecommunication Engineering
questions.stem: Mixer conversion gain definition:
options: IF output power / RF input power
questions.solution: Conversion gain: ratio of IF output to RF input (both in power). Active mixers have gain; passive have conversion loss.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Op-amp slew rate determines:
options: Maximum rate of output voltage change (dV/dt)
questions.solution: SR = max(dV_out/dt). For sine: SR ≥ 2πfV_peak. Slew-rate limited signals look triangular.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inverting amp: Rf=22.0kΩ, Rin=47.0kΩ, Vin=0.1V. Vout:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge

questions.stem: MOSFET — enhancement mode:
options: Vgs > Vt to conduct. Normally off
questions.solution: Enhancement: Vt > 0. Depletion: Vt < 0 (normally on). Square-law in saturation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Op-amp — instrumentation amplifier:
options: High CMRR, high input impedance, gain set by single resistor
questions.solution: INA: 3 op-amp configuration. R_g sets gain. CMRR > 100 dB. Medical, sensor applications.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: MIMO systems:
options: Multiple antennas at transmitter and receiver (spatial multiplexing, diversity)
questions.solution: MIMO: N_t × N_r. Spatial multiplexing: N_min independent streams. Diversity: improved reliability.

questions.stem: Pulse Code Modulation (PCM):
options: Sample → quantize → encode. Quantization error = ±½ LSB. SQNR = 6.02n + 1.76 dB
questions.solution: PCM: Nyquist sampling. n bits/sample. Bit rate = n·f_s. Companding: μ-law, A-law.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Shannon–Hartley theorem gives channel capacity as:
questions.solution: Capacity rises with bandwidth B and signal-to-noise ratio: C = B log₂(1 + SNR) bits/s.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Wien bridge oscillator:
options: Bridge circuit. f = 1/(2πRC). Requires A = 3 (non-inv amp gain)
questions.solution: Wien: frequency-selective positive feedback. AGC for amplitude stabilization.

options: GBW = A_OL × f_c. Constant for VFA. Unity gain freq f_T = GBW

questions.subject: Electronics and Telecommunication Engineering
questions.stem: Delta-sigma ADC: out-of-band noise is shaped by:
questions.solution: NTF = 1/(1+H(z)) where H is loop filter. Pushes quantization noise to high frequencies where it's filtered out.

questions.stem: Current mirror with beta helper resistor: purpose:
questions.solution: Beta helper reduces error from finite beta. Resistor supplies base current from supply, not from reference transistor.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Logarithmic amplifier:
options: V_out ∝ ln(V_in). Uses BJT exponential characteristic. Signal compression
questions.solution: Log amp: dynamic range compression. V_out = -V_T·ln(V_in/I_S·R). For wide dynamic range.

questions.stem: Negative feedback in amplifiers:
options: Reduces gain, increases bandwidth, reduces distortion
questions.solution: Feedback: A_f = A/(1+Aβ). Trade-off: gain for stability, BW, linearity.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 13 kHz tone is sampled at 10 kHz. The alias frequency that appears in the baseband is:
questions.solution: Alias = |f − fs| = |13 − 10| = 3 kHz (since f exceeds fs).

questions.stem: The rectified output waveform shown (every half-cycle is positive) is produced by a:
questions.solution: Both half-cycles appear as positive humps with no gaps — characteristic of full-wave rectification.

questions.stem: Carson's rule estimates FM bandwidth as:
questions.solution: B_FM ≈ 2(Δf + fm), where Δf is peak deviation and fm the highest message frequency.
